Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set up a schedule where I'd study in blocks, maybe four or five hours at a time, but it rarely worked out that way. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time making diagrams and charts from the lecture content, which took longer than just reading. With everything going on and my anxiety being what it is, some weeks I'd be really on top of things, and other weeks I'd barely touch my materials until something urgent came up.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally. I'd have these bursts of productivity where I'd cram in a ton of study hours, then periods where I couldn't bring myself to focus at all. Deadlines definitely dictated when I studied most intensely. I also have extracurriculars that sometimes conflicted with my ideal study times, so I had to be flexible even when I didn't want to be.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, because my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The anxiety would build up if I hadn't checked in for a few days, so that nervous energy would push me to log in even when I didn't feel like studying.
Q4: Yeah, there was a stretch mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, check my phone, then check the LMS before doing anything else. It happened because I was trying to stay on top of ten different online courses, which is overwhelming. The routine helped manage my anxiety a bit, knowing I at least knew what was coming up even if I couldn't always act on it.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly lifesavers for me since I learn best visually. I watched over two hours worth of content because seeing concepts explained with visuals stuck with me way better than reading text. The task completion features helped too because I could see my progress, which gave me small wins when everything else felt overwhelming. Anything with clear visual organization or step-by-step breakdowns worked well for my brain.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. I know I have zero posts there, and it's not because I didn't care about the material. I just get really anxious about putting my thoughts out there for everyone to judge, and with my stress levels already high, I couldn't handle that extra pressure. I also skipped some of the supplementary readings when they were just walls of text without any visual aids.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could visualize the payoff, like understanding a concept that would definitely be tested, I was motivated to engage. Also, anything that helped reduce my anxiety about the course felt valuable, even if it was just organizing my notes or reviewing what I'd already learned to feel more confident.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With ten courses and my mental state being what it is, I had to triage constantly. Required activities had this weight to them that forced action, even when I was exhausted or anxious. Optional stuff got pushed aside because there was always something mandatory breathing down my neck, even though sometimes the optional resources looked genuinely interesting.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet where I could annotate and draw connections visually. I also used my own notebooks extensively, creating mind maps and diagrams that helped me process information. Some of my best study sessions involved textbooks and external YouTube videos explaining concepts in different ways.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours with printed materials spread across my desk, working through problems and making visual summaries, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it from my downloads multiple times while taking detailed notes. My peer interactions number is decent, but a lot of my actual collaboration happened in person or through messaging apps outside the system.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd access a PDF or video once to download it, then never touch the LMS version again. It was easier for me to study from local files because I could organize them my way and access them without internet when I was on the bus or somewhere without good wifi. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt almost impossible. My anxiety would spike, especially around midterms, and I'd feel paralyzed even though I knew I needed to work. The mindfulness intervention they had me try helped somewhat, but honestly my mood afterward was still pretty low. When stress peaked, even logging into the LMS felt like too much because I'd see all the things I was behind on.
Q13: Massively. Having ten online courses meant I was constantly juggling competing deadlines, and fatigue became a real issue. Some weeks I'd be running on fumes, and my LMS usage would drop because I was just surviving. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes, which I know hurt my learning. My extracurriculars added another layer of time pressure that made everything harder to balance.
Q14: Yeah, definitely. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my brain was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. Those sessions were basically useless learning-wise, even though they'd register as activity time. It's frustrating because the effort was there but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly, even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety around public writing just made me freeze up every time I tried to start. I also avoided some of the more challenging problem sets until the last possible moment because facing them meant confronting how much I didn't understand, which triggered more stress. It was this avoidance cycle that I knew was bad but couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, which isn't healthy but it's the truth. I'd have these intense bursts of LMS activity in the days before something was due, then almost nothing afterward. The deadline pressure could cut through my anxiety and force action when nothing else could. My assignment completion rate reflects this pattern, getting things in but often at the last minute after frantic work sessions.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more with similar content. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that area of the course for a while. I'd need time to process it before I could productively use it, which sometimes meant delays in addressing weaknesses.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me anchors to organize around, which I needed. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses meant I couldn't engage deeply with everything. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller chunks because tackling a huge module felt impossible on high-anxiety days. The structure helped me survive but not necessarily thrive.
Q19: Visual content and clear organization made engagement much easier for me. When modules had good diagrams, video explanations, and logical flow, I could actually learn effectively. What made it harder was dense text-heavy sections and anything requiring public participation like discussions. Also, when the path through content wasn't clear, my anxiety about missing something important would spike and I'd sometimes just disengage entirely.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually being able to focus and produce work. Or maybe they're trying to understand material but struggling, so they keep accessing resources without making progress on assignments. It could also be someone who's engaged with learning but has barriers to completing the formal submissions, like perfectionism or fear of failure.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores and assignment completion don't fully reflect how much time I spent studying offline. Someone might download everything once, study extensively from those materials, and show low LMS activity while actually being quite engaged. The system only sees the tip of the iceberg. My attendance was low but I often caught up through recordings and independent study.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you access, and completion rates, which is useful data. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental state behind the activity. They can't tell if someone watched a video while actually learning or while completely zoned out from anxiety. They also miss collaboration that happens outside the platform and the emotional experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, making connections, and the learning feels meaningful rather than just survival mode. For me, real engagement involves creating those visual representations that help concepts click. It's also about feeling mentally present, which is hard when anxiety is high. Engagement is more than activity; it's genuine investment in learning.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much my anxiety affected everything, the internal battle just to log in some days. It can't capture the hours of offline visual note-making that were some of my most productive learning times. It doesn't show that my zero discussion posts came from social anxiety, not disinterest. And it definitely can't show that despite passing, I often felt like I was barely holding on, which shaped every interaction I had with the course.
